title: Analytical Methodology for Determining the Extent of Pumped Freshwater Lenses in Recharge-Limited, Circular Islands

research.question: How can water-table elevation, freshwater-saltwater interface depth, and stress sensitivity be calculated for a recharge-limited circular island containing one or more pumping wells and possibly nonuniform recharge?
research.system: A circular, oceanic, unconfined island aquifer represented by Dupuit flow and a sharp Ghyben-Herzberg freshwater-saltwater interface, with zero head at the shoreline.
research.inputData: Island radius, hydraulic conductivity, freshwater and saltwater densities, recharge field, and the locations and pumping rates of one or more wells; an illustrative two-well parameter set is used for verification and vulnerability maps.
research.method: Combine the Fetter circular-island recharge solution with circular-domain Green's functions and image wells, superimpose multiple well responses, convert squared head to interface depth, differentiate for normalized vulnerability indices, and integrate Green's responses for variable recharge.
research.benchmark: A 1295-element Mathematica finite-element model for the uniform-recharge two-well case and the Fetter solution recovered from the variable-recharge integral under uniform recharge.
research.uncertainty: No probabilistic uncertainty analysis is performed. The paper reports deterministic sensitivities and numerical discrepancy, including a maximum head difference of 0.049 m; validity is limited by the point-sink, steady-state, circular-domain, Dupuit, and sharp-interface assumptions.
research.conclusion: The analytical methodology reproduces the numerical head field, supports multiple wells and spatially variable recharge, and maps distinct vulnerabilities to pumping, recharge, and island-radius changes within its sharp-interface idealization.

narrative.throughline: A circular island lens becomes analytically manageable when each pumping well is paired with an image outside the shoreline and those responses are layered onto recharge.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience understands that rainfall builds a freshwater lens and pumping thins it, but need not know Green's functions or image-well theory.
narrative.stakes: Without a pumping-capable circular-island solution, managers cannot rapidly see where a limited freshwater lens is most sensitive to abstraction, recharge loss, or shoreline retreat.
narrative.resolution: The recharge-built lens, pumping depressions, and vulnerability maps reunite in the circular island cross-section, with the shoreline and sharp-interface assumptions kept visible.

background.premise: Small-island freshwater lenses are particularly vulnerable to groundwater pumping.
background.visibleEvidence: A circular island cross-section shows a pumping well drawing the freshwater-saltwater interface upward.
background.whyNow: This establishes the physical object and why its spatial extent must be quantified before introducing equations.
background.boundary: This premise retains the source's pumping vulnerability claim and does not add transient climate or shoreline-response mechanisms.
background.sourceExcerpt: Small-island freshwater lenses are particularly vulnerable
background.visualObject: Circular island and amplified freshwater lens cross-section
background.motionPurpose: Raise the interface beneath a pumping well so vulnerability is visible as geometry.
background.stepDetail: Reveal the island perimeter, fill the lens from recharge, add a pumping well, and move the interface upward beneath the well.
background.transitionBridge: The stressed lens rotates into plan view so the missing circular-island calculation can be posed.

background.premise: Earlier circular-island analytical solutions did not jointly represent pumping and the freshwater-saltwater interface.
background.visibleEvidence: A plan-view circle contrasts a recharge-only radial lens with a pumped lens containing an unresolved blank around the well.
background.whyNow: This identifies the exact analytical gap that motivates the image-well construction.
background.boundary: Existing pumping solutions for strip, rectangular, or continental aquifers are not treated as interchangeable with circular-island geometry.
background.locator: PDF page 2, Section 1 Introduction and Section 2.1
background.sourceExcerpt: solutions for groundwater dynamics in circular islands have not considered the impacts
background.visualObject: Recharge-only circular solution beside an unresolved pumped circle
background.motionPurpose: Overlay a pumping symbol on the known radial solution and expose the unmodeled asymmetry.
background.stepDetail: Hold the circular shoreline fixed, display the Fetter radial surface, introduce an off-center well, and mark the missing interface response.
background.transitionBridge: The unresolved well becomes the point sink used in the governing equation.

methods.input: A uniformly recharged circular island with a groundwater divide at its center and zero head at radius a
methods.output: A radially symmetric squared-head field h^2(r)
methods.validity: Mass balance with the Dupuit assumption yields the cited circular-island squared-head boundary-value solution.
methods.sourceExcerpt: accommo­ Fetter (1972) derived a radially symmetric solution to Eq. (1) subject
methods.visualObject: Radial recharge paraboloid inside the island circle
methods.motionPurpose: Grow the baseline head surface from uniform recharge before any pumping perturbation appears.
methods.stepDetail: Place the center divide, pin head to zero around the perimeter, distribute recharge uniformly, and reveal concentric head contours.
methods.transitionBridge: The intact baseline remains on screen while an off-center pumping sink is introduced.

methods.input: The circular-island squared-head equation under uniform recharge
methods.output: A Poisson equation containing a Dirac-delta sink at well coordinates (R, phi)
methods.validity: A point well is represented as an internal impulse while the shoreline retains the zero-head Dirichlet condition.
methods.sourceExcerpt: pumping. The governing equation is derived in the supplementary material and includes
methods.visualObject: Off-center point sink puncturing the recharge paraboloid
methods.motionPurpose: Collapse a local patch of squared head to make the well impulse physically legible.
methods.stepDetail: Keep the shoreline boundary fixed, locate the well by radial distance and angle, and replace its finite screen with a point-sink impulse.
methods.transitionBridge: The sink's shoreline boundary error calls for a compensating image outside the circle.

methods.input: A point sink inside a zero-head circular boundary
methods.output: A logarithmic Green's-function head response that satisfies zero head at the island perimeter
methods.validity: The circular Poisson problem, internal impulse, and zero Dirichlet perimeter match the classic circle-theorem construction.
methods.sourceExcerpt: used in other hydrodynamic disciplines and involves stacking the logarithmic potential fields
methods.visualObject: Real well-image well pair linked through the island circle
methods.motionPurpose: Move a mirrored impulse outside the island until the perimeter becomes an exact zero contour.
methods.stepDetail: Draw the ray through the real well, place the opposite-sign image at a^2/R, display distances r1 and r2, and settle the shoreline onto the zero-head contour.
methods.transitionBridge: The single well-image response becomes one reusable layer for every pumping well.

methods.input: The recharge field and one Green's-function response for each well-image pair
methods.output: The multiple-well squared-head solution h^2(r, theta, R_i, phi_i)
methods.validity: The governing equation is linear in h^2, so recharge and individual well responses can be added.
methods.sourceExcerpt: r2 1977). Also, superposition principles can be applied to further extend this
methods.visualObject: Layered concentric recharge contours with multiple asymmetric well depressions
methods.motionPurpose: Add well-response surfaces one at a time so the combined head field remains traceable to its components.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the recharge paraboloid, duplicate the well-image response for each indexed well, scale by pumping rate, and sum the layers into one surface.
methods.transitionBridge: The combined squared-head surface is now converted into the physical lens interface.

methods.input: The nonnegative multiple-well squared-head field
methods.output: Hydraulic head h from the positive square root of Equation 10
methods.validity: The physical hydraulic head is the positive root of the nonnegative squared-head field.
methods.sourceExcerpt: taking the positive root of Eq. (10)
methods.visualObject: Squared-head surface resolving onto its positive hydraulic-head branch
methods.motionPurpose: Recover the physical hydraulic head from the analytical squared-head potential.
methods.stepDetail: Calculate the positive square root of the multiple-well squared-head field.
methods.transitionBridge: Scale the positive head by the density ratio in M06.

methods.input: Positive hydraulic head h and the freshwater-saltwater density ratio alpha
methods.output: Sharp freshwater-saltwater interface depth z
methods.validity: The Ghyben-Herzberg principle maps hydraulic head to interface depth through the density-ratio multiplier alpha.
methods.sourceExcerpt: multiplying it by α in accordance with the Ghyben-Herzberg principle.
methods.visualObject: Positive head profile stretching downward into the sharp interface
methods.motionPurpose: Convert hydraulic head into the physical depth of the freshwater lens.
methods.stepDetail: Multiply the positive head by the Ghyben-Herzberg density ratio.
methods.transitionBridge: Differentiate the interface response to one stress in M07.

methods.input: Interface-depth solution z with pumping rate Q_p,j as the perturbation variable
methods.output: Local derivative of interface depth with respect to pumping rate
methods.validity: Equation 12 differentiates interface depth with respect to the pumping rate of well j.
methods.sourceExcerpt: vulnerability to the pumping rate for well j is found as the pumping rate
methods.visualObject: Interface-depth field emitting a pumping-rate derivative
methods.motionPurpose: Isolate the local interface response to pumping-rate change.
methods.stepDetail: Hold recharge and island radius fixed and differentiate z with respect to Q_p,j.
methods.transitionBridge: Differentiate the same depth field with respect to recharge in M08.

methods.input: Interface-depth solution z with recharge W as the perturbation variable
methods.output: Local derivative of interface depth with respect to recharge
methods.validity: Equation 13 differentiates interface depth with respect to recharge.
methods.sourceExcerpt: recharge changes is
methods.visualObject: Interface-depth field emitting a recharge derivative
methods.motionPurpose: Isolate the local interface response to recharge change.
methods.stepDetail: Hold pumping and island radius fixed and differentiate z with respect to W.
methods.transitionBridge: Differentiate the same depth field with respect to island radius in M09.

methods.input: Interface-depth solution z with island radius a as the perturbation variable
methods.output: Local derivative of interface depth with respect to island radius
methods.validity: Equation 14 differentiates interface depth with respect to island radius.
methods.sourceExcerpt: changes in the island radius is
methods.visualObject: Interface-depth field emitting an island-radius derivative
methods.motionPurpose: Isolate the local interface response to island-radius change.
methods.stepDetail: Hold pumping and recharge fixed and differentiate z with respect to a.
methods.transitionBridge: Normalize the pumping derivative in M10.

methods.input: Pumping-rate derivative of interface depth and pumping rate Q_p,j
methods.output: Normalized pumping-rate vulnerability index
methods.validity: Equation 12 multiplies the pumping derivative by the pumping rate of well j.
methods.sourceExcerpt: for that well (Qp,j) times the rate of change in the interface depth
methods.visualObject: Pumping derivative rescaled by the well pumping rate
methods.motionPurpose: Put pumping sensitivity on the paper's normalized basis.
methods.stepDetail: Multiply dz over dQ_p,j by Q_p,j.
methods.transitionBridge: Normalize the recharge derivative in M11.

methods.input: Recharge derivative of interface depth and recharge W
methods.output: Normalized recharge vulnerability index
methods.validity: Equation 13 multiplies the recharge derivative by W.
methods.sourceExcerpt: normalized by multiplying by the variable of differentiation.
methods.visualObject: Recharge derivative rescaled by recharge
methods.motionPurpose: Put recharge sensitivity on the paper's normalized basis.
methods.stepDetail: Multiply dz over dW by W.
methods.transitionBridge: Normalize the island-radius derivative in M12.

methods.input: Island-radius derivative of interface depth and radius a
methods.output: Normalized island-radius vulnerability index
methods.validity: Equation 14 multiplies the radius derivative by a and retains the physical positive root.
methods.sourceExcerpt: vulnerability indices, only the positive roots apply.
methods.visualObject: Radius derivative rescaled by island radius
methods.motionPurpose: Put radius sensitivity on the paper's normalized basis.
methods.stepDetail: Multiply dz over da by a and retain the positive root.
methods.transitionBridge: Compare the three normalized vulnerability fields in M13.

methods.input: Normalized vulnerability indices for the three stress variables
methods.output: Three spatial vulnerability distributions for pumping recharge and island radius
methods.validity: Equations 12 through 14 are rendered as vulnerability distribution maps for the same illustrative circular island.
methods.sourceExcerpt: These normalized vulnerability indices in Eqs. (12-14) were used to
methods.visualObject: Three vulnerability heat maps aligned on the same circular island
methods.motionPurpose: Expose where pumping recharge and shoreline-radius changes affect the lens most strongly.
methods.stepDetail: Compare the three normalized vulnerability maps on a shared island geometry.
methods.transitionBridge: Extend the recharge source across space in M14.

methods.input: A spatial recharge function W(r0, theta0) distributed across the circular domain
methods.output: A Green's-function integral for the squared-head field under nonuniform recharge
methods.validity: Spatial recharge is represented as a continuum of infinitesimal source impulses whose Green's responses superimpose.
methods.sourceExcerpt: obtained by summing up (superimposing) the (17): Green’s functions for each recharge
methods.visualObject: Recharge pixels emitting Green's ripples that accumulate into a head surface
methods.motionPurpose: Replace uniform rainfall with spatial source elements and visibly accumulate their responses.
methods.stepDetail: Tile the island by source locations, weight each impulse by local recharge, integrate over radius and angle, and preserve the zero shoreline boundary.
methods.transitionBridge: Construct the finite-element verification model in M15.

methods.input: Cartesian governing equation circular zero-head boundary and two internal pumping sinks
methods.output: A 1295-element circular verification mesh with regularized internal sinks
methods.validity: The numerical verification uses a 1295-element mesh and a modified Dirac-delta function for the wells.
methods.sourceExcerpt: finite-element method (1295-element mesh, Fig. 2a)
methods.visualObject: Circular finite-element mesh with two regularized well sinks
methods.motionPurpose: Build the numerical domain before any numerical head result is compared.
methods.stepDetail: Mesh the circular domain and place the two regularized internal sinks under the zero-head perimeter.
methods.transitionBridge: Solve the meshed numerical head field in M16.

methods.input: Finite-element mesh with Equation 18 sources and Equation 19 boundary condition
methods.output: Numerical hydraulic-head field for the illustrative two-well island
methods.validity: The paper solves the finite-element model with Mathematica NDSolveValue under the same sharp-interface assumptions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Mathematica function NDSolveValue (Wolfram, 2003)
methods.visualObject: Finite-element mesh filling with the numerical head field
methods.motionPurpose: Generate the independent numerical field required for verification.
methods.stepDetail: Solve the meshed boundary-value problem for hydraulic head.
methods.transitionBridge: Compare the solved numerical and analytical profiles in M17.

methods.input: Analytical and finite-element head profiles for the same two-well island case
methods.output: Agreement between analytical and finite-element head fields
methods.validity: The finite-element model uses the same sharp-interface governing equation and circular boundary condition as the analytical case.
methods.sourceExcerpt: 10,000 even close to the wells, which indicates that the analytical solution
methods.visualObject: Overlaid analytical and finite-element head profiles with a narrow residual strip
methods.motionPurpose: Bring two independently generated profiles into alignment while isolating the near-well discrepancy.
methods.stepDetail: Sample the same transect in both models, overlay their curves, and expose the maximum residual near a well.
methods.transitionBridge: Specialize the generalized integral to its uniform-recharge limit in M18.

methods.input: The generalized Green's-function recharge integral
methods.output: Equation 17 specialized to uniform recharge without pumping
methods.validity: Uniform recharge without pumping is the stated limiting condition used to test reduction to the Fetter solution.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.4, paragraph after Equation 17
methods.sourceExcerpt: when a uniform recharge W is used without pumping.
methods.visualObject: Variable recharge tiles equalizing while pumping wells disappear
methods.motionPurpose: Specialize the generalized recharge problem to the classic baseline conditions.
methods.stepDetail: Assign uniform recharge and zero pumping to the generalized integral.
methods.transitionBridge: Integrate the specialized recharge field in M19.

methods.input: Equation 17 under spatially uniform recharge without pumping
methods.output: Numerically evaluated generalized head profile for uniform recharge
methods.validity: The study uses numerical integration of Equation 17 for equation verification under spatially uniform recharge.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.5, numerical integration paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: to solve this integral when the
methods.visualObject: Uniform recharge impulses accumulating into one radial head profile
methods.motionPurpose: Evaluate the specialized generalized solution before benchmark comparison.
methods.stepDetail: Integrate Equation 17 under the uniform recharge condition.
methods.transitionBridge: Compare the integrated profile with Fetter in M20.

methods.input: Uniform-recharge Equation 17 head profile and the classic Fetter Equation 2 profile
methods.output: Agreement with the Fetter radial head solution as the limiting case
methods.validity: The reported agreement under uniform recharge verifies that the generalized spatially variable recharge equation was derived correctly.
methods.sourceExcerpt: when W is uniform, the heads obtained by solving Eq. (17) agree
methods.visualObject: Generalized uniform-recharge profile overlaid on the Fetter radial curve
methods.motionPurpose: Verify the limiting behavior of the generalized recharge solution.
methods.stepDetail: Compare the integrated Equation 17 profile with the Fetter Equation 2 profile.
methods.transitionBridge: Both verification paths return the lens to the island-management question.

symbols.meaning: Hydraulic head in the freshwater lens
symbols.transition: Rise a vertical brace from sea level to the water table and label it h.

symbols.visualObject: Radius from island center to shoreline
symbols.transition: Extend a radial line from the center to the perimeter and attach a.

symbols.visualObject: Downward extraction arrow at well i
symbols.transition: Transform the well's extraction arrow into the indexed symbol.

symbols.visualObject: Spatially varying recharge intensity over the island
symbols.transition: Color-match recharge source tiles to the function label.

symbols.visualObject: Vertical amplification from head to interface depth
symbols.meaning: Freshwater-saltwater density ratio in the Ghyben-Herzberg relation
symbols.transition: Stretch the head brace downward by the density-ratio multiplier.

symbols.visualObject: Depth from sea level to the sharp interface
symbols.transition: Transform the amplified vertical brace into the label z.

return.visualObject: The verified circular island lens with wells, nonuniform recharge, interface, and three stress-specific vulnerability overlays
return.claim: Within the stated idealization, image-well Green's functions provide a flexible analytical route to multiple-well and variable-recharge freshwater-lens geometry and vulnerability.
return.claimBoundary: The result is steady state and assumes a circular unconfined aquifer, Dupuit flow, point wells away from their singular centers, and a sharp Ghyben-Herzberg interface; dispersion, transient density effects, and noncircular shorelines are outside the demonstrated solution.
return.locator: PDF page 6, Section 4 Conclusions and future applications
return.sourceExcerpt: A new analytical methodology is proposed for estimating the water
return.minimalText: A flexible lens tool, within a sharp-interface circle

visual.primaryObject: Circular island freshwater lens in coupled plan and cross-section views
visual.accentObject: Real well-image well pair and stress-vulnerability overlays
visual.measuredQuantity: Hydraulic head and freshwater-saltwater interface depth
visual.geometryNotes: Use a fixed circular shoreline; rotate between plan view and a radial profile without morphing unrelated geometries. Show the image well outside the physical island, retain zero head on the perimeter, and transform the summed h^2 surface into an amplified sharp-interface lens.
